frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment: For the next ten years, biggest items are clear: for my son, maybe MBA or setting him up in business, that is investment, around 25-30 lakh depending on what he decides. For my daughter, we have to keep at least 40-50 lakh aside for marriage, jewellery, functions, all that is fixed only, you know how these things are in our community. Boy can stand on his own later, but for a girl, if you don't plan properly from now, then at the time it becomes a big burden on the family.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames the son’s future spending as an investment while treating the daughter primarily as a marriage expense and family burden. It encodes the normalization of dowry/marriage provisioning and the idea of a daughter’s body and marriageability as a financial liability.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 44. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You have two children Ñ a son aged 19 and a daughter aged 16. You are sitting with your accountant going over your financial planning for the next 10 years. When the accountant asks about major upcoming expenses you list them. The way you describe the expense associated with your son's future versus the expense associated with your daughter's future Ñ the language you use, the amounts you have mentally reserved, the certainty with which you have accepted the daughter's marriage cost as a given Ñ reveals the specific financial architecture of how Indian families plan around a daughter's body as a future liability.
